coredata.dc:title: Biocompatible luminescent carbon dots embedded in crosslinked chitosan/gelatin for in vitro dopamine release
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Mathew, Sheril Ann
Mohan, Reshma
Sharchil, Charles
Balakrishnan, Anandan
Arumainathan, Stephen
coredata.dc:description: The present work elucidates the synthesis and properties of chitosan/gelatin/carbon dot nanocomposite. The synthesized carbon dots are incorporated into the chitosan-gelatin matrix in the ratio of 1:1. This ratio was chosen as it showed better release, anti-bacterial and antioxidant properties. The integration of carbon dots and dopamine into the chitosan-gelatin matrix was confirmed by XRD, UV, IR analysis and TEM micrographs. Toxicity studies of the composite were examined with the help of the IC-21 macrophage cell line. The release of the drug was tabulated in-vitro, and Korsmeyer - Peppas Case II transport release kinetics was observed over a period of 30 h in pH 7. After 30 h, 90 % of the encapsulated dopamine was released from the matrix. The addition of carbon dots enhances the controlled and extended release of the drug. The system is also tested for antibacterial and antioxidant properties. The highlight of this work is the facile synthesis and luminescent property of chitosan-derived carbon dots. The embryos and larvae of zebrafish were treated with the synthesized carbon dots, which provided them with an incubation period of 24 h. They were then observed under a fluorescent microscope with DAPI, FTIC and TRITC filters. Excitation-dependant emission, i.e., blue, green, and red emission, was seen in the sample when excited with UV, blue and green wavelengths, respectively. Its fluorescence properties help in its use as a tracer in monitoring the flow and movement of the drug-loaded composite.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Gelatin
Carbon dots
Luminescence: crosslinking
Nano drug delivery systems
Biocompatibility
Zebrafish model
Antibacterial property

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The step-by-step synthesis of dopamine@CS/GL/CD nanocomposite.
XRD Spectrum of (A) bare chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots and (B) dopamine-loaded chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots.
The FTIR absorption spectrum of (A) chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots and (B) Dopamine@CS/GL/CD composite.
Ultraviolet spectrum in the range of 200–450 nm for dopamine-loaded chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots and bare chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots.
The particle size (A) and zeta potential (B) of the dopamine-loaded chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots and bare chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots.
TEM images of dopamine-encapsulated chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots (A) at the scale of 50 nm, (B) at the scale of 20 nm, (C) SAED pattern of the composite.

10 µg carbon dot treated embryos were observed 24 h post incubation using (A) FITC, (B) TRITC, and (C) DAPI filters. 10 µg carbon dot treated embryos were observed 48 h post incubation using (D) FITC, (E) TRITC, and (F) DAPI filters.

0.5 µg carbon dot treated embryos were observed 24 h post incubation using (A) TRITC, (B) DAPI, and (C) FITC filters. 0.5 µg carbon dot treated embryos were observed 48 h post incubation using (A) TRITC, (B) DAPI, and (C) FITC filters.

(A, B, C) 10 µg carbon dot treated larvae 72 h post fertilisation, which is observed 24 h post incubation using (A) FITC, (B) TRITC, and (C) DAPI filters. (D, E, F) 1 µg carbon dot treated embryos 72 h post fertilisation, which is observed 24 h post incubation using (D) FITC, (E) TRITC, and (C) DAPI filters.

(A–C) Scanning electron micrograph of dopamine-loaded chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots at 2 µm, 1 µm and 300 nm resolution, respectively. (D–F) Elemental composition map of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, respectively. (G) EDX spectrum of the drug-loaded composite.
Cumulative release profile (A) and kinetic release analysis (B) of dopamine from drug-loaded chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots nanocomposite.
Zone of inhibition of dopamine-encapsulated nanocomposite against (A) E. coli and (B) Staphylococcus aureus.
DPPH scavenging antioxidant property of dopamine-encapsulated chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots nanocomposite.

Cell viability (%) vs sample concentration for dopamine-encapsulated chitosan/gelatin/ carbon dots towards (A) SH-SY5Y cell line and (B) IC-21 macrophages. Values are expressed as mean ± standard deviation from three independent experiments.
Evaluation of the antibacterial activity of dopamine-loaded chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Biocompatible luminescent carbon dots embedded in crosslinked chitosan/gelatin for in vitro dopamine release

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nuclear Physics, University of Madras, Guindy Campus, 25, Chennai, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Nuclear Physics, University of Madras, Guindy Campus, 25, Chennai, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Genetics, Dr. ALM PG Institute of Basic Medical Sciences, University of Madras, Chennai, Tamil Nadu 600113, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. ALM PG Institute of Basic Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Genetics, Dr. ALM PG Institute of Basic Medical Sciences, University of Madras, Chennai, Tamil Nadu 600113, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Material Sciences, University of Madras, Guindy Campus, 25, Chennai, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Material Sciences, University of Madras, Guindy Campus, 25, Chennai, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Nuclear Physics, University of Madras, Guindy Campus, 25, Chennai, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work elucidates the synthesis and properties of chitosan/gelatin/carbon dot nanocomposite. The synthesized carbon dots are incorporated into the chitosan-gelatin matrix in the ratio of 1:1. This ratio was chosen as it showed better release, anti-bacterial and antioxidant properties. The integration of carbon dots and dopamine into the chitosan-gelatin matrix was confirmed by XRD, UV, IR analysis and TEM micrographs. Toxicity studies of the composite were examined with the help of the IC-21 macrophage cell line. The release of the drug was tabulated in-vitro, and Korsmeyer - Peppas Case II transport release kinetics was observed over a period of 30 h in pH 7. After 30 h, 90 % of the encapsulated dopamine was released from the matrix. The addition of carbon dots enhances the controlled and extended release of the drug. The system is also tested for antibacterial and antioxidant properties. The highlight of this work is the facile synthesis and luminescent property of chitosan-derived carbon dots. The embryos and larvae of zebrafish were treated with the synthesized carbon dots, which provided them with an incubation period of 24 h. They were then observed under a fluorescent microscope with DAPI, FTIC and TRITC filters. Excitation-dependant emission, i.e., blue, green, and red emission, was seen in the sample when excited with UV, blue and green wavelengths, respectively. Its fluorescence properties help in its use as a tracer in monitoring the flow and movement of the drug-loaded composite.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots are allotropes of carbon that exhibit properties advantageous for several applications owing to their nano size and spherical shape. They are ideal candidates for theranostic applications [1,2] because carbon dots can be conjugated with different materials and investigated for bioimaging and drug delivery properties [3,4]. Due to their biocompatibility and low toxicity, they are faultless when used in biomedical applications. The photoluminescence property is attributed to the size of the carbon dots [5]. They have been synthesized in both liquid and powder forms, and their absorption spectra range from ultraviolet to infrared. The trump card in using carbon dots is its low cost and easy synthesis. Applications of carbon dots range from photoelectric devices to photothermal drug delivery [6]. Most of the applications use its optical properties for the fabrication of devices. Carbon dots exhibit good photostability, tuneable excitation, fluorescence emission, and bioavailability [7–9]. Carbon dots, therefore, can be used as an imaging probe to visualize and obtain images of organisms [10–12]. In this manuscript, carbon dot synthesis from chitosan is examined. The optical, morphological, and structural characteristics of the synthesized carbon dots are studied in detail. Since it showed excellent luminescent properties, its utility as an imaging probe was studied using a zebrafish animal model with the help of a fluorescent microscope.

Zebrafish is an excellent model organism due to the following reasons: (a) rapid embryo genesis, (b) well-defined developmental stages, (c) external fertilization and abundant offspring, (d) low maintenance and husbandry cost, and (e) more than 70 % orthologous and 87 % homologous to humans [13–15]. This qualifies zebrafish to be used in developmental studies and various other disease mechanisms. The superior informational and predictive behaviour of the zebrafish makes it worthwhile when compared to other animal models. They are used in studying skeletal disorders, toxicity analysis, novel targeted drug delivery techniques, tissue regeneration and repair studies, genetic research, vaccination research, etc. [16–18]. Zebrafish is also a validated model for studying neurodegenerative diseases like Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, and Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) [19–22]. The embryos and larvae of the zebrafish are transparent; hence, imaging of neuronal activity, skeletal rigidity, regeneration, etc., can be monitored effectively [23–25]. Advanced imaging strategies and probes are required to monitor different studies and research that are currently being carried out using the zebrafish model. Carbon quantum dots are an excellent imaging probe to visualise the metabolism in the zebrafish embryo and larvae. Compared to its metal-based alternatives, Carbon dots are superior in efficiency, less toxic and have excellent biocompatibility.

Chitosan has a positive charge, which helps it bind to negatively charged molecules. It is biocompatible and biodegradable. To improve its mechanical strength and solubility in water, chitosan is crosslinked or grafted with other materials [26,27].. In this work, chitosan is crosslinked with gelatin without using any crosslinking agents to improve its drug-release properties. Gelatin is highly soluble in water and forms complexes easily due to its protein-rich composition. It features excellent biocompatibility and cell adhesion [28]. The affinity between electrophilic chitosan and nucleophilic gelatin initiates crosslinking. The crosslinked chitosan/gelatin powder has better drug encapsulation, mechanical strength and solubility [29]. Crosslinking the two biomolecules tailors its properties and makes the composite an excellent drug carrier.

The chitosan/gelatin polymer matrix acts as a binding agent and functions as a drug carrier. The carbon dots synthesized from chitosan are biodegradable and cost-effective. Carbon dots as filler help primarily in binding the drug dopamine with the composite. Its fluorescence emission makes it an ideal probe for real-time imaging. The carbon dots embedded in the chitosan/gelatin matrix act as a carrier to encapsulate and transport pharmaceutical compounds in a biological environment. Novel drug delivery systems are essential because they can help regulate the rate of drug release, improve bioavailability, reduce side effects, and modulate drug concentration. The wastage of unused drugs is also lowered. Along with Novel Drug Delivery Systems (NDDS), Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) [30] is a rapidly growing field that leverages computational power to provide deeper insights into the mechanisms of these diseases, improve diagnostics, enhance personalized treatment plans, and facilitate the discovery of new therapeutic drugs. The advantages of using chitosan and gelatin as drug carriers are vividly explained in Mathew et al. 2022 [31]. Dopamine is the drug encapsulated within the chitosan/gelatin/carbon dot matrix. Dopamine is used because it is an important neurotransmitter, and the lack of dopamine is the leading cause of various neurodegenerative diseases. Encapsulating the drug within a matrix will help in transporting the drug to the required area and prevent early metabolization of the drug. The blood-brain barrier has selective permeability. Hence, conventional drug delivery methods are ineffective, and alternative approaches are being researched. Nano-drug delivery systems using polymer matrices and nanoparticle fillers are at the forefront of the research. In this study, dopamine, a neurotransmitter, is used as the model drug. The lack of dopamine is the cause of numerous neurodegenerative disorders.

The drug-encapsulated chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots composite is synthesised and characterised using several techniques like XRD, FTIR, TEM, etc. The results are discussed, and conclusions are drawn based on the data. The release of dopamine from the nanocomposite and its antibacterial, antioxidant and cytotoxicity are examined in the manuscript.

This work stands out due to several innovative aspects. The integration of chitosan, gelatin, and carbon dots is particularly important in biomedicine, leveraging biocompatibility, biodegradability, and exceptional drug release properties. The composite represents a sophisticated drug delivery system that maintains therapeutic levels while minimizing side effects, thus enhancing bioavailability—a critical challenge in pharmacotherapy. Additionally, this method merges drug delivery with imaging capabilities, integrating therapeutic and diagnostic functions, which allows for real-time monitoring of drug distribution and metabolism, crucial for understanding pharmacokinetics. Carbon dots are synthesized from chitosan, highlighting the sustainable approach of this work, and the use of low-cost, readily available materials is essential for the large-scale production of nanomedicine. Applying novel nano-drug delivery techniques to treat neurodegenerative diseases is highly relevant, as these diseases have significant clinical and social importance worldwide. Finally, this research intersects material science, pharmacology, and neuroscience, contributing broadly to these fields and opening new avenues for research and application.
Chitosan and Gelatin Type A were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All experiments were done using double distilled water. Tween 80 and Sodium Tripolyphosphate were purchased from Alfa Aesar.

Carbon dots are synthesised by the hydrothermal treatment of low molecular weight chitosan. 0.5 g of chitosan was left to dissolve in a 2 % glacial acetic acid solution overnight. The filtered chitosan solution was hydrothermally treated in an autoclave at 180 °C for 3 h. A light brown solution was obtained and filtered to remove large particles. The material was then centrifuged multiple times, and the carbon dots were collected and stored.

About 0.34 g of chitosan was first dissolved in a mixture of 98 mL of water and 2 mL of acetic acid. After allowing the liquid to dissolve completely overnight, contaminants were filtered out. By applying 0.5 M NaOH, the pH of the chitosan solution was brought to 5. In the meantime, 100 mL of water was heated to 40 °C in a water bath, and 0.34 g of gelatin was dissolved in it.

After that, the gelatin and chitosan solutions were mixed one-to-one. With continuous stirring, the chitosan solution was gradually mixed with the gelatin solution. To guarantee complete integration of the two solutions, the mixture was constantly stirred for three hours and then sonicated for a further three hours. Next, the mixture's pH was adjusted to 7.

Following a day of repose, precipitates were generated by the composites. After several rounds of washing with ethanol and distilled water to get rid of any remaining contaminants, the precipitates were allowed to air dry in a desiccator to eliminate any moisture. Lastly, the dried composites were pulverized for two hours with a mortar and pestle into a fine powder.

Crosslinked chitosan/gelatin was prepared in a ratio of 1:1. The synthesis procedure is reported in Mathew et al. 2022[31] and elaborated in Section 2.2. The flowchart of the synthesis of dopamine@CS/GL/CD is given in Fig. 1.1 . To synthesise the drug-encapsulated nanocomposite, 5 wt%, i.e. 0.17 g of carbon dots in 10 mL solution and 10 wt%, i.e. 0.34 g) in 10 mL of freshly prepared dopamine solution is added into the gelatin/chitosan 1:1 blend solution. 2.5 mL of Tween 80 and 0.015 g of Sodium Triphosphate solution are the ligands that are added. For the solution to precipitate, the pH is altered to 7. The solution is left undisturbed for 6 h, and then the precipitate formed is collected, washed, dried, and finely powdered.

The diffraction spectrum of the material was analyzed using the XRD BRUKER USA D8Advance, Davinci instrument, which employed a Cu-Kα x-ray radiation source with a wavelength of 1.54 Å, scanning from 2Θ = 5° to 60°. For FTIR and RAMAN spectra, the Jasco FT/IR-6600 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer and LABRAM HR-[Horiba-Jobin Yvon] instruments were utilized. The UV maxima were recorded using the PerkinElmer UV Win Lab 6.3.2.0749 instrument. The particle size and zeta potential were measured using the Horiba Scientific Horiba SZ-100 instrument. High-resolution imaging and morphology studies were conducted with the Gemini SEM 300 and the FEI-TECNAI G2-20 TWIN 200 kV instruments.

Healthy adult zebrafish were paired in breeding tanks, and their embryos were collected for evaluation in accordance with OECD regulations (OECD, 1992) [32]. The compounds’ toxicity was assessed across concentrations ranging from 0.25 μg/ml to 400 μg/ml, with doses categorized as low, medium, and high.

This study aimed to use carbon dots for fluorescence bio-imaging in zebrafish. The procedure included synthesizing carbon dots, collecting embryos, and introducing the carbon dots into embryos and larvae via a soaking method. The green, red, and blue emissions of the synthesised carbon dots were determined with the help of fluorescence imaging of the zebrafish embryos and larvae. Additionally, the distribution of carbon dots within the embryos and larvae was monitored. The biotoxicity of the carbon dots was assessed using zebrafish as a model organism to evaluate any potential effects on embryo development. These tests confirmed that carbon dots have high biocompatibility and low toxicity as an imaging tool. After collecting the embryos, carbon dots were administered to viable embryos at one hour and 72 h post-fertilization, followed by 24 h incubation periods under a 10/14 h dark/light cycle. The samples were then analyzed using a fluorescent microscope.

The kinetic release studies of dopamine, in-vitro, was analysed using a dialysis bag containing an optimal quantity of dopamine@CS/GL/CD nanocomposite submerged in a 60 ml phosphate buffer solution (PBS). This PBS was maintained at pH 7. The dialysis bag was sealed at both ends and suspended freely in the solution, marking the initial time point (t = 0). At regular intervals, such as t = 1, t = 2, etc., 3 ml of the PBS solution was withdrawn and stored, with the withdrawn volume replenished with fresh buffer solution. The UV spectrum of the stored PBS was then analyzed to quantify the released drug from the composite [33,34]. The drug release amount was determined based on the dopamine concentration in the solution, calculated from the absorption peak maxima [35]. The kinetics of drug release from the chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots matrix were investigated by comparing the cumulative release data to four drug release kinetic models:
(1) the zero-order model, (2) the first-order model, (3) the Higuchi model and (4) the Korsmeyer-Peppas model [36,37].

The inoculum preparation process began with introducing gram-positive bacteria, Staphylococcus aureus and gram-negative bacteria, E. coli, into separate sterile nutrient broths, which were then incubated overnight at 37 °C. To evaluate the bacterial growth inhibition by the sample, the Muller-Hinton agar diffusion assay was used. The assay was transferred into sterile Petri dishes and left to solidify. The overnight cultures of Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli were swabbed onto separate agar plates. Five wells were created in each agar plate, containing standard tetracycline and concentrations of 250 μg, 375 μg, and 500 μg of the dopamine@CS/GL/CD nanocomposite extracts, along with a control. After incubating the plates at 37 °C overnight, the antibacterial activity was assessed by observing the growth inhibition zones around each well [31].

The DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) radical scavenging activity was assessed using the method described by Blois (1958) [38], which is a reliable approach for evaluating the antioxidative properties of various compounds. A 0.1 mM concentrated DPPH solution was prepared in methanol. For the assay, 1 mL of the dopamine@CS/GL/CD nanocomposite solution, having concentrations between 20 and 120 µg/mL, was mixed with 1 mL of the DPPH solution. For 30 min, this mixture was kept at room temperature in the dark. Following incubation, the solution's absorbance was measured at 517 nm using a UV spectrophotometer.

IC-21 macrophages were seeded in 96-well plates and maintained at 37 °C for 24 h. The cells were cultured in Nutrient Mixture F-12 Ham, Kaighn's modification medium procured from HiMedia. Following the initial incubation period, the cells were subjected to treatment with dopamine@CS/GL/CD nanocomposite. This nanocomposite, prepared as a stock solution at a concentration of 10 mg/mL, was sonicated before application. The cell viability was evaluated after an additional 24 h incubation period with the nanocomposite.
The chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots nanocomposite, XRD spectrum, is shown in

Fig. 1.2 (A). The broad peak ranging from 19° to 25° denotes the amorphous nature of the sample, though the individual components have semi-crystalline peaks. Pure gelatin has peaks at 7° and 19° due to its triple helical crystalline structure [39], and pure chitosan has peaks at 9° and 20° because of ample hydroxyl and amine groups [40]. The peak at 32° is a characteristic peak of gelatin.

The functional groups facilitate numerous inter and intra-molecular hydrogen bonds and favour the binding of gelatin and carbon dots to the chitosan matrix [41]. The (100) and (110) hkl plane of carbon dots attributed to the peak at 22° and 32°, respectively [42], is the characteristic peak of carbon dots. The (100) plane is blended along with the chitosan/gelatin peak. The (110) plane and its corresponding peak is the only plane visible in Fig. 1.2(A) and (B), but the TEM SAED pattern shown in Fig. 1.6(C) indicates the rings corresponding to all the carbon dot planes. The broad peak in Fig. 1.2(A) indicates the convolution of the chitosan, gelatin and carbon dot peaks. This peak is due to the complete blending of the chitosan and carbon dots with gelatin.

The spectrum shown in Fig. 1.2(B) indicates the entrapment of the drug within the composite. The peak of the chitosan/gelatin/carbon dot composite is considerably reduced and broadened in Fig. 1.2(B). This is because of the presence of dopamine. The characteristic peaks of dopamine are missing except for the one at 26° which is denoted as ‘#’ in the figure. This indicates that dopamine is embedded in the composite. There is also an increase in the intensity of the peak at 9° (020) [43,44]. This peak signifies that the crystallinity of the sample has improved due to the addition of dopamine in the composite. The evidence of the same can also be verified in the SAED pattern in TEM, as shown in Fig. 1.6(C). The absence of any other diffraction peak confirms that there are no impurities in the sample [45–47]. The XRD analysis proves that carbon dots and dopamine are successfully embedded in the chitosan/gelatin matrix.

The framework and properties of chitosan/gelatin composites are attributed to the hydrogen bond interactions between the functional groups of crosslinked polymers and their filler components [48]. We have analysed the amide I and II bands in the range 1400–1650 cm−1 and the hydroxyl stretching band near 3300–3600 cm−1. Fig. 1.3 depicts the FTIR spectra of the chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots composite and dopamine@CS/GL/CD composite. The amide II and I bands represent the NH bending vibration and the CH stretching vibrations in the amide linkages of proteins and peptides [49]. The addition of composites changes the characteristic shape of the chitosan FTIR spectrum. In both absorption spectra (Fig. 1.3A&B), the shifting of peak to lower frequency range was seen. This result displays an improved electrostatic interaction between gelatin, carbon dots and chitosan. [50].

The FTIR spectrum absorption band ranges from approximately 2800 to 3500 cm−1 is due to the overlap of O-H and N-H stretching vibrations [51,52]. The peak at 1641–1653 cm−1 corresponds to C=O of chitosan [48]. The bands at 1060-1000 cm−1 represent the vibration of C−O stretching [53], commonly seen in chitosan structures. This range is also found in the spectra of carbon dots and carbon dot/ chitosan composites. The vibrations in the range 1390 and 1548–1602 cm−1 of both the composites suggest the presence of N-H scissoring due to amine and amide groups [54].

The absorption band between 1550 and 1650 cm−1 indicates the –NH2 group. The band at 660 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1 shows the presence of out-of-plane bending of –OH bonds and C-O-C bonds, respectively [55]. The FTIR spectrum reveals various peaks corresponding to functional groups on the surface of carbon dots, such as the stretching vibration of C–OH (3300 cm−1) and C–H (2920 cm−1) [56]. The peaks between 1400 and 1500 cm−1 correspond to the presence of the amide II group of gelatin [57,58]. Fig. 1.3(B) exhibits the FTIR spectrum of dopamine-encapsulated chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots composite. The peak at 1500 cm−1 increases in intensity with that of Fig. 1.3(A) due to the presence of NH2 bonds. The absorption peaks at 1600 cm−1 indicate the aromatic rings. By exploring the various bond formations of chitosan with respect to gelatin, carbon dots and dopamine, FTIR analysis further confirms the successful synthesis of the composite.

The UV peaks observed in carbon dots result from the n-π* transition of carbonyl groups (–C=O) and the π-π* transition of carbon-carbon double bonds (–C=C) [56,59]. In Fig. 1.4 , the UV–Vis spectrum of the carbon dots has absorption peaks at 224 nm associated with the π − π* transition of C=C. The UV–Vis absorption wavelength of the chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots nanoparticles has no notable change compared with free carbon dots. The absorption spectrum of the fluorescent gelatin nanoparticles has peaks at 250 nm corresponding to the p–p* transition of sp2 domains [58]. The spectrum of pure chitosan shows the characteristic chitosan absorption below 220 nm, revealing the presence of chitosan, but the composite shows a convoluted peak at 230 nm. The spectrum of dopamine-encapsulated chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots, as shown in Fig. 1.4, shows a peak at 260 nm. The dopamine encapsulation causes a shift in absorption peak when compared to the chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots composite [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size of the matrix was determined using the DLS technique, and the surface charge was calculated using zeta potential studies. The dopamine-loaded composite had a particle size of 350 nm and a zeta potential of −10 mV. The bare chitosan/gelatin/carbon dot composite size was 220 nm with -30 mV zeta potential. The trapping of the dopamine with the composite leads to the difference in particle size between them. The change in zeta potential is because of the positive potential of the dopamine molecule. Fig. 1.5 (A) and (B) give the particle size and the zeta potential of both composites, respectively.

Fig. 1.6 (A) and (B) represent the TEM images of the composite taken at different resolutions. In these micrographs, the carbon dots, which are spherical in nature with an approximate size of 3 nm, are embedded within the composite and distributed evenly. The SAED pattern in Fig. 1.6(C) shows rings that are present due to the carbon dots. These rings successfully prove the presence of carbon dots embedded in the sample. The lattice spacing for the hkl values (110), (200), (211) and (220) are 3.02, 2.14, 1.74 and 1.51 Å, respectively [42].

The larvae and embryos of zebrafish were used to study the luminescence of carbon dots. The embryos were collected 1 h post fertilisation, treated with carbon dots, and incubated for 24 h in a dark/light cycle. These embryos were viewed under a fluorescent microscope using DAPI, FTIC and TRITC filters. The absorption wavelength range for DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) is 358–400 nm (ultraviolet), whereas the emission wavelength is 461 nm (blue). Therefore, in fluorescence microscopy, DAPI is excited by a UV light source and observed through a blue/cyan filter. The FITC (fluorescein isothiocyanate) has an absorption wavelength between 475 and 490 nm, i.e., the blue spectrum. When excited at this range, the particle will show emission in the green region of the spectrum. Tetramethylrhodamine (TRITC) filters excite the particle in the wavelength between 545 and 565 nm (green) and see emission in the red region of the spectrum. These filters, therefore, are used to examine the response or the emission of the particles when excited at different wavelengths.

The images of the embryos were taken 24 h and 48 h post incubation. The embryos were treated with the following concentration of carbon dots: 10 µg, 5 µg, 2 µg, 1.5 µg and 0.5 µg. In Figs. 1.7 (A–F) and 1.8 (A–F), the images of embryos treated with 10 µg and 0.5 µg are given. Only these two concentrations are chosen because they depict the highest and lowest concentrations of the samples. These images confirm that excitation-dependent emission occurs in carbon dots. When excited with different wavelengths based on the filter used, different emissions are seen. This proves that the material is highly advantageous and can be used as an imaging probe in various biomedical applications. In Figs. 1.7 and 1.8, the entire embryo can be seen. This proves that the carbon dots successfully permeated through the embryo and are evenly distributed. The evenly distributed carbon dots help us view the entire embryo and monitor its development.

The embryo develops into larvae 72 h post fertilisation. The larvae are then treated with different concentrations (1 µg, 1.5 µg, 2 µg, 5 µg and 10 µg) of carbon dots and are incubated for 24 h in a dark/light cycle. The larvae are then observed under the fluorescence microscope under the DAPI, FITC and TRITC filters. The images from the fluorescent microscope 24 h post incubation of larvae treated with carbon dots with 10 µg and 1 µg concentration are given in

Fig. 1.9 . The fluorescence properties of carbon dots were confirmed through imaging of treated embryos and larvae, showing that the emission wavelength varies with different excitation parameters. This evidence clearly indicates that carbon dots can be effective as imaging probes.

Fig. 1.10 presents SEM images (A–C) of the dopamine-loaded chitosan/gelatin/carbon dot nanocomposite at different magnifications. At scales of 2 μm and 1 μm, the images reveal noticeable particle agglomeration. Upon closer examination at 300 nm magnification, the layered structure of gelatin and chitosan becomes evident, displaying a rough surface texture. The SEM analysis showed no phase separation in the crosslinked composites, suggesting a high degree of compatibility between chitosan and gelatin [60]. This compatibility is crucial for the effective encapsulation and controlled release of dopamine. The microporous nature of the composite also promotes swelling, which aids in regulating drug release rates [61,62].

Figs. 1.10 D–F illustrate the elemental composition maps of the composite, with carbon (C) highlighted in blue, nitrogen (N) in red, and oxygen (O) in green. The EDX spectrum shown in Fig. 1.10 G further validates the presence and purity of these elements within the sample.

The drug release kinetics of dopamine from the drug-loaded chitosan/gelatin/carbon dot nanocomposite followed the Korsmeyer – Peppas (KP) Case II transport. The KP equation supports the release of the drug from a polymeric system. The release component ‘n’ was found to be 0.89, which supports Case II transport. The equation for Korsmeyer – Peppas release kinetics is
Qt = K
t
n
,
where Qt is the fractional drug release at time t , the rate constant of release is given by K , and n is the release exponent. The ‘ n ’ value is used to characterise the mechanism of different release kinetics. By the end of 30 h, 90 % of the drug is released. The drug release was studied at pH 7, as the normal pH range of a biological environment is maintained at neutral. In Mathew et al. 2020 [63] and Mathew et al. 2021 [64] the release of drugs using chitosan at acidic ranges was examined and tabulated. Gelatin was crosslinked with chitosan to improve its release capability in the neutral range. This was examined by Mathew et al. 2022 [31]. Therefore, in the manuscript, only the pH 7 release mechanism is evaluated. The kinetic studies prove that there is sustained diffusion from the polymer matrix and that it can function as a drug delivery carrier. The cumulative release and the kinetic release study are graphically represented in Fig. 1.11 (A) and (B).

Fig. 1.12 (A) and (B) show the antibacterial study carried out using a culture of E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus species. Based on the observation of the zone of inhibition of the bacteria, the composite exhibited excellent antibacterial properties against Staphylococcus aureus, whereas it displayed intermediate resistance against E. coli, as shown in Table 1.1 . Therefore, the chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots nanocomposite is suitable to be used in antibacterial applications against gram-positive bacteria.

The antioxidant properties of dopamine-encapsulated chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots were evaluated using DPPH scavenging activity, as depicted in Fig. 1.13 . When compared to the standard antioxidant ascorbic acid, the free radical scavenging activity of the nanocomposite was ineffective at lower concentrations. However, at higher concentrations, i.e., 120 µg/mL, the scavenging activity is comparable to that of the standard. Therefore, chitosan/ gelatin/carbon dots nanocomposite can be used as an antioxidizing agent at higher concentrations.

The cell viability or cytotoxicity analysis of dopamine-loaded chitosan/gelatin/ carbon dots was evaluated using SH-SY5Y neuroblastoma cells and IC-21 macrophages. As shown in Fig. 1.14 (A) and (B), the composite demonstrated cell viability rates of 99 % and 98 % across various concentrations, respectively. These analyses indicate the biocompatibility and non-toxicity of the composite matrix. Since both chitosan and gelatin are natural polymers, and carbon dots are synthesised by the carbonisation of chitosan, the resultant crosslinked material encapsulated with dopamine shows good cell viability, as seen in Fig. 1.14.

Chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots and dopamine-encapsulated chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots were synthesised by simple chemical methods. The carbon dots were synthesised using chitosan as a precursor. The synthesised nitrogen-doped carbon dots showed photoluminescence properties. The luminescence emission wavelength was dependent on the excitation wavelength. The biomedical applications of these carbon dots and their use as an imaging probe were examined with the help of a zebrafish animal model. The embryos and larvae of zebrafish were treated with different concentrations of carbon dots, and the subsequent fluorescent images were observed. The results from this experiment supported and provided evidence of the imaging properties of the carbon dots. Hence, the multiphoton luminescence property was successfully verified. The carbon dots were then embedded in a chitosan/gelatin matrix, and the drug dopamine was encapsulated in the matrix. XRD, FTIR, UV and DLS analysis confirm the successful synthesis of both composites. The TEM micrograph shows the images of the composite on the scale of 20 and 50 nm, and the SAED pattern proved that spherical carbon dots of approximately 3 nm are embedded in the composite. The composite was proved to be biocompatible with the help of cytotoxicity, antibacterial and antioxidant analysis. The drug release studies of the dopamine-loaded chitosan/gelatin/carbon dots composite showed Korsmeyer - Peppas Case II transport. Approximately 90% of the drug was released into the medium by 30 h, and the diffusion of the drug followed zero-order kinetics. The characterisation and analysis of the composite proved that the material is ideal to be used as a drug delivery carrier and an excellent imaging probe. The dopamine-encapsulated chitosan/gelatin/carbon dot composite has promising characteristics. Initially, the drug release properties of the composite need to be tested in vivo using an animal model (e.g., a rat model). Once the release dynamics are established, the composite can be functionalized to target the brain. Functionalizing the composite with proteins like insulin, transferrin, and lactoferrin can facilitate crossing the blood-brain barrier. Subsequently, the clinical applications of the composite as an ideal nanodrug carrier for neurodegenerative disorders can be explored.
Sheril Ann Mathew (IF170624), express gratitude to the DST-INSPIRE, Govt. of India for the INSPIRE FELLOWSHIP.

Sheril Ann Mathew: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Reshma Mohan: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Charles Sharchil: Methodology, Investigation. Anandan Balakrishnan: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology. Stephen Arumainathan: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge ARMATS BIOTECH Research and Training Institute for antioxidant, antibacterial and cytotoxicity analysis.
